title: Cohort Profitability Drift

content.statement: New growth is not always as good as past growth.
content.question: Are you tracking each business cohort separately, or are you hiding bad cohorts in good aggregates?
content.equation: CPD = Current Cohort Profitability - Prior Cohort Profitability
sop: Group customers into cohorts by the month or quarter they joined.
Calculate profitability per customer for your most recent cohort (see calculation, page 6).
Calculate profitability per customer for a prior cohort from 6-12 months ago (see calculation, page 6).
Subtract prior cohort profitability from current cohort profitability to see the drift (see calculation, page 6).
If drift is negative, investigate acquisition channel, pricing, or service quality changes between the two periods before scaling further.
content.explainer: Growth can rot at the edges while aggregates look healthy. Cohorts are how you detect it.
questions: Are you tracking each business cohort separately, or are you hiding bad cohorts in good aggregates?
Is the customer you signed last month more or less profitable than the one you signed a year ago, and do you actually know?
If your newest cohort is underperforming, is it because the market shifted or because your acquisition got sloppier?

definitions.def: A group of customers who started in the same period.

content.equation_example: Current Cohort Profitability $120 per customer, Prior Cohort Profitability $150 per customer. $120 minus $150 equals negative $30. Negative or falling means newer customers are less valuable than older ones, a warning sign hidden inside your growth. Positive or rising means your newest customers are actually your best ones.
content.closing_statement: Healthy aggregates can hide cohorts already going bad.

caption:
Principle Nº 58: Cohort Profitability Drift

New growth is not always as good as past growth. Growth can rot at the edges while aggregates look healthy.

Ask yourself: Are you tracking each business cohort separately, or are you hiding bad cohorts in good aggregates?
